name: Tanuki Full
description: The Tanuki, particularly when referred to as 'Tanuki Full', embodies the mischievous and jovial spirit of Japanese folklore. More than just a raccoon dog, the Tanuki possesses magical abilities, most notably shapeshifting, which it uses for pranks and sometimes for more benevolent purposes. Often depicted with a large belly (used as a drum) and an even larger scrotum (symbolizing prosperity), the Tanuki is a symbol of good fortune and carefree living. They are known for their love of sake and their generally sociable nature, often depicted in art and stories as amiable tricksters who enjoy playing jokes on humans. While generally harmless, a Tanuki can be formidable if provoked, using their magic to defend themselves or their territory. They are deeply connected to nature and are often associated with forests, mountains, and rural areas. The Tanuki's playful nature and association with prosperity have made them a beloved figure in Japanese culture, appearing in numerous stories, songs, and works of art. Their image is often used in advertising and as decorative figures to bring good luck and cheerfulness. They represent a unique blend of humor, magic, and connection to the natural world, making them a captivating and enduring part of Japanese mythology.
subtitle: Mischievous shapeshifting raccoon dog spirit
appearance: The Tanuki Full is typically depicted as a stout, raccoon dog-like creature with brown fur and a distinctive black mask around its eyes. They have short legs, a bushy tail, and a round belly. The most notable feature is their disproportionately large scrotum, which is often depicted as being comically large and used for various purposes, such as a drum, a parasol, or even a boat. They often wear a straw hat and carry a sake bottle, further emphasizing their jovial and carefree nature. Their expression is usually cheerful and mischievous, reflecting their playful personality.
habitat: Tanuki are primarily found in forests, mountains, and rural areas throughout Japan. They are closely associated with nature and are often considered guardians of the forests. They prefer areas with plenty of vegetation and access to water sources.
behavior: Tanuki are generally nocturnal creatures, spending their days sleeping in their dens and emerging at night to forage for food and play pranks. They are known for their sociable nature and are often depicted in groups, enjoying sake and music together. While generally harmless, they can be mischievous and enjoy playing tricks on humans, often using their shapeshifting abilities to create illusions and cause confusion. They are also known for their love of food and drink, and are often depicted as being greedy and indulgent.

keyMyths.title: Bunbuku Chagama
keyMyths.description: A Tanuki transforms into a teapot to repay a kind man who saved it. The teapot performs tricks and earns the man great wealth before returning to its natural form.

keyMyths.title: Kachi-kachi Yama
keyMyths.description: A vengeful rabbit tricks and punishes a Tanuki for its cruelty towards an old woman. The Tanuki is ultimately defeated through the rabbit's cunning and trickery.

sources.author: Michael Ashkenazi
